reasoning_model_1: The model successfully generates the content with high accuracy and natural fluency; there are no mispronunciations or mechanical artifacts. However, regarding the CORE paralinguistic requirement (Pitch), the model fails to adhere to the instruction. The audio begins at a medium pitch [0:00-0:04] ('The steak... delicious'), but instead of rising, it follows a standard declarative prosody where the pitch remains relatively flat and then drops significantly at the end of the sentence [0:07-0:09] ('...truffle sauce'). This falling intonation is the opposite of the requested 'gradually raising to a very high pitch'. Therefore, while the speech is intelligible, it does not meet the specific dynamic adjustment criteria.
reasoning_model_2: The model generates the text with perfect accuracy and excellent fluency. Crucially, it demonstrates a strong adherence to the pitch instruction. The audio starts at a neutral/medium pitch [0:00-0:03]. As the narrative shifts at [0:04] ('Wait, I just had a brilliant idea'), the pitch notably steps up. The model successfully manages a continuous gradient of rising pitch, culminating in a very high pitch on the final phrase [0:07-0:09] ('...truffle sauce!'), almost resembling an interrogative or highly excited contour. The transition is smooth and matches the 'gradually raising' constraint without unnatural jumps.
model_comparison: Both models achieve perfect scores on content accuracy and fluency, producing natural-sounding human speech. The decisive difference lies in the CORE paralinguistic feature: Pitch. Model 1 completely ignores the instruction to raise the pitch, defaulting to a standard reading with a falling pitch at the end. Model 2 successfully implements the difficult constraint of a gradual pitch increase across a long sentence, ending on the required high note. Model 2 is the clear winner for following the complex prompt instructions.
